courses.course_title: Geosystems: An Introduction to Earth & Environmental Sciences
courses.department: Department of Geological Sciences (administered by Geological Sciences; presented jointly with Archaeology, Environmental & Geographical Sciences, and Oceanography)
courses.prerequisites: At least 50% for NSC Geography, or at least 60% for NSC Physical Science or Life Sciences. Preference given to students majoring in Archaeology, Environmental and Geographical Science, Geology or Oceanography.
courses.course_outline: This course aims to develop a broad understanding of how the Earth works. Over its >4.5 billion-year history, the Earth has been shaped by chemical, physical and biological processes. The course demonstrates why decoding the rock record of deep-time events is critical for explaining past, present and future environmental changes and the distribution of natural resources. It examines the interplay of terrestrial, marine, atmospheric and cryospheric processes within the Earth system, with emphasis on monitoring essential variables for understanding climate variability and change. The course also reviews human evolution and how Homo sapiens became the dominant cause of global environmental change, and explores social risks and vulnerabilities in response to the Earth's biophysical processes. It concludes with the concepts of environmental hazard, risk, resilience, monitoring and adaptation.
courses.notes: Students majoring in Archaeology, EGS, Geology or Oceanography are required to attend one half-day excursion.
courses.dp_requirements: An average of 40% on all marked classwork and tests. Submission of 80% of weekly assignments in each of the four sections (GEO, AGE, SEA, EGS). Attendance of the field trip is compulsory for students majoring in Archaeology, EGS, Geology or Oceanography.
courses.assessment: Marked classwork including class test/s counts 50%; a 3-hour written examination counts 50%. A sub-minimum of 40% in the class mark is required to write the theory examination paper. Supplementary examinations offered in July.

courses.course_outline: This course introduces students to the Geology major and covers the essentials of the discipline: crystals and minerals; igneous and metamorphic rocks; structural geology; mineral deposits and economic geology; palaeontology; and the interpretation of geological maps. A three-day field trip to the Western Cape serves as an introduction to field geology.
courses.dp_requirements: An average of 30% in all marked classwork and tests.
courses.assessment: Class tests count 35%; field reports count 15%; one 2-hour theory examination written in November counts 50%. A sub-minimum of 40% is required in the theory examination paper.

courses.prerequisites: GEO1009F and GEO1006S; CEM1000W or equivalent.
courses.course_outline: This course covers the fundamentals of physical and chemical mineralogy and its application to igneous and metamorphic rocks as a basis for senior courses in petrology. Inter-related sections include: crystallography and crystal optics (including a brief introduction to X-ray crystallography); mineralogy (chemical, physical and optical properties of selected groups of rock-forming minerals, and theory and practice of identifying minerals by polarising microscope); phase diagrams (interpretation of simple phase diagrams relevant to igneous and metamorphic rocks); classification and petrography of igneous rocks (physical processes of magma differentiation and the relationship between chemical composition and modal mineralogy); classification and petrography of metamorphic rocks (types of metamorphism, metamorphic textures and mineral assemblages).
courses.dp_requirements: Attendance at 80% of practicals and an average of 30% in all marked classwork and tests.
courses.assessment: Marked classwork including tests counts 20%; one 2-hour practical examination in June counts 30%; one 2-hour theory examination in June counts 50%. Sub-minima of 40% are required in both practical and theory examination papers.

courses.course_outline: This course builds on the previous mineralogy course and explores the physical structure of the Earth and the physical processes involved in sedimentary rock formation and rock deformation. Topics include: transport and deposition of sediments; sedimentary textures and structure; siliciclastic, carbonate, evaporitic and other sedimentary rocks; earthquakes, stress, displacement and strain; brittle and ductile deformation; interpretation of geological maps and cross sections; introduction to tectonics and global geophysics.
courses.dp_requirements: An average of 30% in marked classwork, and attendance at 80% of practicals.
courses.assessment: Class tests and practicals count 25%; one 2-hour practical examination in November counts 30%; one 2-hour theory examination in November counts 45%. Sub-minima of 40% are required in both practical and theory examination papers.

courses.course_outline: This is a field-based course that introduces techniques used to identify, describe and document rocks in the field and for interpreting their inter-relationships, with the view to producing geological maps, stratigraphic logs and structural sections. Techniques covered include: mineralogical and textural descriptions of rocks using a hand-lens; measurement of attitude of bedding using compass and clinometer; measurement, description and interpretation of depositional and deformational structures; stereo plots; interpretation and use of aerial photographs; identifying contact relationships; GPS positioning. Course material is taught over four separate field camps spread over two years of study.
courses.lecture_times: None. Course is delivered entirely through field camps.
courses.assessment: Maps and reports count 70%; three 2-hour practical examinations in June and November count 30%.

courses.course_outline: This course covers key concepts in igneous, metamorphic and sedimentary petrology in combination with structural geology. Topics include: interpreting major and trace element and isotope variations in igneous rocks; origin and evolution of the major magma series; thermodynamics, kinetics and chemography of metamorphic reactions; tectonic setting of metamorphic terrains; principles of interpretation and classification of continental and marine sedimentary environments; kinematic principles, deformation mechanisms, microstructure, faulting and tectonic geomorphology.
courses.dp_requirements: Attendance at 80% of practicals and an average of 30% in all marked classwork and tests.
courses.assessment: Classwork counts 20%; one 4-hour practical examination written in June counts 30%; one 3-hour theory examination written in June counts 50%. Sub-minima of 40% required in both practical and theory examination papers.

courses.course_outline: This course covers the development of the oceanic and continental rock record and associated ore deposits. Topics include: the principles of stratigraphy with examples drawn from the South African rock record; the methods and procedures involved in dating rocks; the genesis of economic mineral deposits, their microscopic textures, and their valuation and exploitation; geophysical techniques.
courses.dp_requirements: An average of 30% in all marked classwork and class tests.
courses.assessment: Practicals and tests count 25%; one 3-hour theory examination written in November counts 45%; two 2-hour practical examinations written in November count 30%. Sub-minima of 40% required in both practical and theory examination papers.

courses.prerequisites: A BSc degree with a major in Geology; first qualifying courses in Chemistry and Mathematics. A first qualifying course in Physics is recommended. Registrations are limited to 16; acceptance at the discretion of the Head of Department based on quality of final year results, material covered in undergraduate curriculum and referee reports. Preference given to UCT graduates.
courses.course_outline: Students are required to take 7 compulsory modules covering: Geochemistry (including Isotope and Marine Geochemistry), Geophysics, Economic Geology, Igneous, Metamorphic and Mantle Petrology, Palaeontology, Quaternary Geology, Petroleum Geology, Sedimentary Basins, Tectonics, Geological Data Interpretation & Analysis, and Scientific Communication. In addition, each student undertakes a supervised research project (approved by the Honours course co-ordinator and Head of Department). All students are required to attend a two-week fieldtrip held during the year.
courses.notes: Course code GEO4000W carries no NQF credit value itself. Students are concurrently registered for GEO4003W (coursework component, 120 NQF credits) and GEO4004W (research project, 40 NQF credits). Entrance is limited to 16 students.
courses.assessment: Modules are examined mid-year and in October (the latter includes a 3-hour General Exam). Examinations count 45%; practical and assignment work done during the year counts 30%; research project counts 25%. Sub-minima required: 40% for overall examination mark and 50% for the research project. Coursework and project codes must each be passed separately for the award of the degree.

courses.prerequisites: As for GEO4000W. Students undertaking a geochemical or analytical geochemistry project may elect to graduate in Geochemistry, subject to approval of the Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: As for GEO4000W (Geology Honours). Students undertaking a geochemical or analytical geochemistry project elect to graduate under this code.
courses.notes: Course code GEO4001W carries no NQF credit value itself. Students are concurrently registered for GEO4005W (coursework component, 120 NQF credits) and GEO4006W (research project, 40 NQF credits).

courses.course_outline: This course consists of an investigation of an approved topic chosen for intensive study by the candidate, culminating in the submission of a dissertation. The dissertation shall demonstrate the successful completion of a programme of training in research methods, a thorough understanding of the scientific principles underlying the research and an appropriate acquaintance with the relevant literature. It must be clearly presented and conform to the standards of the department and faculty. The dissertation will usually consist of a report detailing the conduct and analysis of the results of research performed under the close guidance of a suitably qualified supervisor/s. It should be well-conceived, acknowledge earlier research in the field, and demonstrate the ability to undertake a substantial and informed piece of research and to collect, organise and analyse material.

courses.prerequisites: EGS5012W (refer to the Department of Environmental and Geographical Science section).
courses.course_outline: The minor dissertation is based on a three- to six-month supervised research project, to be submitted at the end of January, with the possibility of extension to June of the following year.
courses.assessment: The minor dissertation must be presented for formal examination. Coursework and minor dissertation each count 50% towards the degree; each must be passed separately for the award of the degree.

courses.course_outline: The PhD is a research degree on an advanced topic under supervision. The thesis must constitute a substantial contribution to knowledge in the chosen subject, must show evidence of original investigation and give a full statement of the literature on the subject. The PhD degree demands that the candidate is able to conduct independent research on their own initiative, demonstrating that they are at the academic forefront in the topic selected, that the work is original and that it advances knowledge in the relevant field. Prospective candidates are referred to the rules for the PhD degree in Book 3, General Rules and Policies.
